name: Mindstrong Health
summary: Backed by prominent psychiatrists and Silicon Valley investors, Mindstrong built smartphone-based biomarkers to predict mental health crises but repeatedly pivoted its business model and never found a sustainable customer base.
lesson: Sophisticated science and elite pedigree cannot substitute for a validated, repeatable go-to-market motion.

name: Cerebral Inc.
summary: Cerebral scaled rapidly by prescribing controlled substances like Adderall via telehealth, but faced DOJ and FTC investigations, an SEC probe, mass layoffs, and the departure of its founder amid allegations of prioritizing growth over safe prescribing.
lesson: Growth incentives that conflict with clinical safety in regulated healthcare will eventually trigger regulatory intervention.

name: Done Global (Done Health)
summary: An ADHD-focused telehealth startup whose founder and clinical president were criminally charged by the DOJ in 2024-2025 for allegedly running a scheme to distribute Adderall prescriptions without legitimate medical need.
lesson: Optimizing prescription volume through algorithmic shortcuts in controlled-substance care invites criminal liability.
source: Department of Justice press release, Bloomberg

name: Quartet Health
summary: Quartet Health built a platform connecting primary care providers with mental health specialists and insurers, but wound down operations in 2023 after failing to reach profitability, selling some assets to Telefirst.
lesson: Multi-sided care-coordination platforms require enormous capital to reach network density before revenue catches up to burn.

name: Pear Therapeutics
summary: A pioneer of FDA-cleared prescription digital therapeutics for substance use disorder and other conditions, Pear filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in April 2023 after failing to secure durable insurance reimbursement for its apps.
lesson: FDA clearance alone does not create a market; without payer reimbursement pathways, prescription digital therapeutics cannot generate durable revenue.

name: Akili Interactive
summary: Akili developed EndeavorRx, the first FDA-cleared video-game treatment for pediatric ADHD, but after going public via SPAC its stock collapsed and it was taken private in 2024 at a fraction of its once multi-billion-dollar valuation.
lesson: A first-of-its-kind regulatory clearance generates headlines, but commercial adoption and reimbursement take far longer than public-market investors will wait.

name: Lantern
summary: Lantern offered CBT-based digital coaching for anxiety and body image issues but shut down in 2018 after failing to scale beyond early adopters into a sustainable B2B or consumer business.
lesson: Early clinical promise in a niche digital-CBT product does not guarantee a large enough addressable market to justify venture funding.

name: Joyable
summary: Joyable offered a CBT-based coaching app for social anxiety, pivoted its model multiple times, and ultimately shut down in 2020 as larger, better-funded mental health platforms captured the market.
lesson: In a fast-consolidating category, repeated pivots burn runway faster than they find product-market fit.

name: Limbix
summary: Limbix built a digital therapeutic for adolescent depression and pursued FDA clearance, but wound down its clinical product efforts in 2022 as reimbursement and adoption hurdles proved too steep.
lesson: Pediatric digital therapeutics face an especially narrow reimbursement path that can strand even well-funded, clinically-validated products.

name: Better Therapeutics
summary: A prescription digital therapeutics company (public via SPAC) focused on behavioral treatment for cardiometabolic disease, Better Therapeutics filed for bankruptcy and ceased operations in mid-2024 after running out of cash.
lesson: Digital therapeutics companies that go public before proving reimbursement economics face a brutal public-market reckoning.

name: Ontrak (formerly Catasys)
summary: Ontrak provided AI-driven behavioral health coaching for health plan members, but its stock collapsed over 90% in 2021 after its largest health-plan client terminated its contract, triggering years of restructuring and delisting risk.
lesson: Extreme customer concentration in enterprise health-plan contracts creates existential single-point-of-failure risk.

name: Field Trip Health
summary: A network of ketamine-assisted psychotherapy clinics that went public in 2020, Field Trip Health sold off its clinic assets and effectively wound down after its psychedelic drug-development spinout failed to progress and cash ran out.
lesson: Brick-and-mortar clinical care businesses layered with speculative drug-development bets carry two capital-intensive burn profiles at once.

name: Lykos Therapeutics (MAPS PBC)
summary: The spinout from MAPS developing MDMA-assisted therapy for PTSD saw the FDA reject its new drug application in 2024 over data integrity and trial design concerns, followed by layoffs and leadership departures.
lesson: Even a first-in-class therapy with strong efficacy signals can be halted by regulators over trial conduct and unblinding concerns.
source: FDA complete response letter coverage, STAT News

name: Wellnite
summary: Wellnite was a telehealth platform offering online therapy and psychiatric medication management that shut down in 2023 amid the broader contraction in venture-backed mental health telehealth.
lesson: Undifferentiated telehealth-therapy startups without a defensible niche struggled to survive the post-2022 funding pullback.

name: Daybreak Health
summary: Daybreak Health provided school-partnered teletherapy for teenagers but ceased clinical operations in 2024 after struggling to build a sustainable reimbursement model around school district partnerships.
lesson: Selling into school districts brings long sales cycles and thin, unpredictable per-student economics that are hard to scale profitably.

name: Woebot Health
summary: A pioneer of AI chatbot-based mental health support, Woebot Health shut down its consumer-facing app in 2024 to pivot fully toward enterprise and health-system partnerships, effectively ending its original product.
lesson: Direct-to-consumer AI chatbot engagement rarely converts into a monetizable business without a clinical or enterprise wrapper.

name: Simple Habit
summary: A meditation app competing directly with Calm and Headspace, Simple Habit struggled to differentiate and effectively ceased independent operations amid intense market saturation in consumer meditation apps.
lesson: Consumer wellness apps entering a category already dominated by two well-capitalized leaders face brutal customer-acquisition-cost economics.

name: 18percent
summary: 18percent was an anonymous peer-support chat platform for mental health that shut down in 2020, citing the difficulty of moderating peer support safely and sustainably at scale.
lesson: Peer-to-peer mental health support platforms carry acute safety and moderation liabilities that are hard to fund and staff at seed stage.

name: Ginger.io (early pivot)
closed_note: original mood-tracking product discontinued before Ginger relaunched as Ginger Health
summary: Ginger's original consumer mood-tracking and behavioral analytics app was discontinued around 2016 as the company pivoted entirely to enterprise on-demand behavioral health coaching, which later became the successful Ginger Health.
lesson: Sometimes a failed consumer product is the necessary detour to discover a much larger enterprise opportunity.

name: Talkspace
summary: Talkspace built one of the largest text and video-based online therapy platforms in the US, went public via SPAC in 2021, and has grown into a profitable, insurance-covered telehealth business.
lesson: Building insurance reimbursement into the core model early allowed Talkspace to scale past pure-cash-pay competitors.

name: BetterHelp
summary: BetterHelp became the largest online therapy marketplace in the world under Teladoc Health ownership, driven by massive direct-to-consumer marketing spend and a large therapist network.
lesson: Being acquired early by a larger healthcare platform gave BetterHelp the balance sheet to out-market every subsequent competitor.

name: Headspace (Headspace Health)
summary: Headspace grew from a meditation app into a broad mental health company after merging with clinical provider Ginger in 2021 to form Headspace Health, combining consumer wellness with clinical care.
lesson: Merging a strong consumer brand with a clinical care delivery network created a defensible full-spectrum mental health offering.

name: Calm
summary: Calm became the leading consumer meditation and sleep app, achieving profitability and a multi-billion-dollar valuation through a freemium subscription model and celebrity partnerships.
lesson: A disciplined freemium subscription funnel and strong brand content let Calm reach profitability without needing continuous outside capital.

name: Lyra Health
summary: Lyra Health built an enterprise mental health benefits platform selling directly to large employers, becoming one of the most highly valued mental health startups by combining a vetted provider network with outcomes measurement.
lesson: Selling mental health benefits directly to enterprise HR buyers created durable, high-value contracts and switching costs that consumer apps lack.

name: Spring Health
summary: Spring Health provides employer-sponsored mental health benefits using a precision-matching algorithm to connect employees with the right level of care, scaling to hundreds of enterprise customers.
lesson: Data-driven care matching became a defensible wedge in the crowded employer mental health benefits market.

name: Modern Health
summary: Modern Health built a full-spectrum employer mental health benefits platform combining coaching, therapy, and digital tools, reaching unicorn status by 2021.
lesson: Offering a tiered care continuum (self-guided to coaching to therapy) inside one employer benefit made adoption easier for HR buyers.

name: Two Chairs
summary: Two Chairs built a hybrid in-person and virtual therapy practice using a proprietary matching algorithm, expanding across multiple US states while maintaining high clinical quality standards.
lesson: Combining measurement-based matching technology with a real clinical practice model built defensible quality differentiation versus pure marketplaces.

name: Alma
summary: Alma built a membership platform helping independent therapists manage insurance billing and practice operations, becoming a unicorn by streamlining insurance-based therapy access.
lesson: Solving the insurance billing pain point for independent therapists created a sticky two-sided marketplace without owning clinical risk directly.

name: Headway
summary: Headway built a marketplace connecting therapists who accept insurance with patients, rapidly scaling to unicorn status by simplifying insurance credentialing and billing for providers.
lesson: Removing insurance friction for both therapists and patients unlocked a much larger addressable market than cash-pay-only competitors.

name: SonderMind
summary: SonderMind built an insurance-based therapist marketplace with measurement-based care tools, reaching unicorn valuation in 2023 while expanding across multiple states.
lesson: Pairing outcomes measurement with an insurance-covered marketplace helped SonderMind win payer partnerships that pure cash-pay apps could not.

name: Grow Therapy
summary: Grow Therapy provides back-office and credentialing infrastructure for therapists to accept insurance, achieving unicorn status by 2023 by rapidly onboarding thousands of independent providers.
lesson: Focusing narrowly on provider-side insurance infrastructure let Grow Therapy scale supply quickly without owning clinical delivery risk.

name: Talkiatry
summary: Talkiatry built an in-network telepsychiatry practice employing its own psychiatrists, scaling rapidly by taking insurance from day one rather than operating as a cash-pay marketplace.
lesson: Employing psychiatrists directly and billing insurance natively avoided the credentialing friction that slowed many marketplace competitors.

name: Brightline
summary: Brightline provides pediatric and family-focused virtual behavioral health care, growing its footprint across employer and health plan contracts targeting children and teens.
lesson: Specializing in pediatric behavioral health filled a severely underserved niche that generalist platforms largely ignored.

name: Rula Health
summary: Rula (formerly known under different branding) built an insurance-covered mental health marketplace connecting patients to therapists and psychiatrists, reaching unicorn status in 2024.
lesson: Rapid payer-contract expansion combined with provider recruiting allowed Rula to scale supply and demand in tandem.

name: LifeStance Health
summary: LifeStance Health rolled up outpatient mental health practices nationwide and went public in 2021, becoming one of the largest outpatient mental health provider networks in the US.
lesson: A disciplined roll-up strategy in a highly fragmented outpatient therapy market created national scale that individual practices could not achieve alone.

name: AbleTo
summary: AbleTo built an evidence-based virtual behavioral health program integrated with health plans, and was acquired by Optum in 2021 as part of UnitedHealth's push into behavioral health.
lesson: Deep integration with health plan care management programs turned AbleTo into an attractive acquisition target for a payer-owned health services giant.

name: Genoa Healthcare
summary: Genoa Healthcare built a specialty pharmacy network embedded in behavioral health clinics, and was acquired by Optum in 2018, becoming a key medication access channel for community mental health.
lesson: Embedding pharmacy services directly inside community mental health clinics created an operational moat that pure telehealth players couldn't replicate.

name: Karuna Therapeutics
funding: Acquired by Bristol Myers Squibb for $14B (2023)
summary: Karuna Therapeutics developed KarXT, a novel antipsychotic for schizophrenia with a new mechanism of action, and was acquired by Bristol Myers Squibb in 2023 for $14 billion after strong Phase 3 trial results.
lesson: A genuinely novel mechanism of action for a decades-stagnant disease category can command an enormous acquisition premium.

name: Insight Timer
summary: Insight Timer grew a large free library of meditation content into one of the most-downloaded meditation apps globally, monetizing via a premium subscription tier atop a large organic user base.
lesson: A generous free tier with a large content marketplace built organic scale that paid-acquisition competitors struggled to match.

name: Ten Percent Happier
summary: Ten Percent Happier grew from a bestselling book and podcast into a meditation and coaching app, sustaining growth through a trusted media brand rather than paid acquisition alone.
lesson: Leveraging an existing trusted media brand meaningfully lowered customer acquisition costs versus pure app-store competition.

name: 7 Cups
summary: 7 Cups built a large network of trained volunteer listeners offering free emotional support chat, sustaining itself for over a decade as a low-cost complement to professional therapy.
lesson: A volunteer-powered peer support model can achieve capital-efficient scale that professional-therapist marketplaces cannot match on cost.

name: Charlie Health
summary: Charlie Health provides virtual intensive outpatient programs for teens and young adults in acute mental health crisis, scaling rapidly to fill a gap between outpatient therapy and inpatient hospitalization.
lesson: Targeting the underserved 'step-down' level of care between therapy and hospitalization captured demand competitors overlooked.

name: Brightside Health
summary: Brightside Health provides insurance-covered virtual psychiatry and therapy with an outcomes-focused, data-driven treatment model, expanding coverage across most US states.
lesson: Publishing clinical outcomes data helped Brightside win payer trust and differentiate from less rigorous telehealth competitors.

name: NeuroFlow
summary: NeuroFlow built a behavioral health integration platform embedding mental health screening and remote monitoring tools into primary care and health system workflows.
lesson: Embedding measurement tools inside existing clinical workflows, rather than building a standalone consumer app, drove durable enterprise adoption.

name: Array Behavioral Care
summary: Array Behavioral Care (formerly InSight Telepsychiatry) built one of the longest-running telepsychiatry networks, partnering with hospitals and health systems to deliver acute and outpatient psychiatric care.
lesson: Deep, long-standing hospital and health-system partnerships built durable revenue well before the 2020 telehealth boom.

name: Big Health
summary: Big Health developed Sleepio and Daylight, digital CBT programs for insomnia and anxiety, and secured multiple employer and health plan contracts as clinically validated digital therapeutics.
lesson: Rigorous published clinical trial evidence gave Big Health's digital therapeutics credibility that helped it win payer contracts other apps lacked.

name: Happify Health
summary: Happify Health built a science-based digital wellbeing platform with gamified exercises for stress and anxiety, selling to enterprises and health plans alongside its consumer app.
lesson: Pairing consumer engagement mechanics with health-plan distribution created two complementary revenue channels.

name: Equip Health
summary: Equip Health built a virtual, evidence-based eating disorder treatment program using a multidisciplinary care team model, scaling nationally with strong insurance partnerships.
lesson: Focusing on a single, severely underserved diagnosis with an evidence-based multidisciplinary model built strong payer trust quickly.

name: Mantra Health
summary: Mantra Health partners with colleges and universities to provide teletherapy and telepsychiatry to students, filling capacity gaps in campus counseling centers.
lesson: Selling into higher-education institutions as a capacity extension, rather than a replacement, avoided political resistance from campus counseling staff.

name: Osmind
summary: Osmind built an EHR and outcomes-tracking platform purpose-built for ketamine and psychedelic-assisted therapy clinics, becoming the leading software infrastructure provider for the emerging category.
lesson: Building infrastructure software for an emerging clinical category can succeed even while the underlying clinical companies using it struggle.

name: Mindbloom
summary: Mindbloom built an at-home, clinician-supported ketamine therapy program for depression and anxiety, scaling across multiple states while maintaining a lighter operating model than clinic-based competitors.
lesson: An at-home, tele-supervised model kept capital intensity far lower than brick-and-mortar ketamine clinic competitors like Field Trip Health.

name: Concert Health
summary: Concert Health provides collaborative care behavioral health services embedded within primary care practices, billing through Medicare's collaborative care reimbursement codes.
lesson: Aligning the business model directly with a specific CMS reimbursement code created a durable, defensible revenue mechanism.

name: SilverCloud Health
summary: SilverCloud Health built evidence-based digital CBT programs used by the UK's NHS and US health systems, and was acquired by Amwell in 2021 to expand its behavioral health offering.
lesson: Deep NHS clinical validation gave SilverCloud a credibility moat that translated into a strong acquisition outcome in the US market.

name: MDLive
summary: MDLive offered virtual primary care, psychiatry and therapy visits and was acquired by Cigna's Evernorth in 2021, integrating telehealth including behavioral health directly into a major payer.
lesson: Building a broad telehealth platform that included behavioral health made MDLive a strategic acquisition target for a payer seeking vertical integration.

name: Wysa
summary: Wysa built an AI chatbot-based emotional support app that expanded into clinically validated, NHS-approved digital mental health support and enterprise employer wellness contracts.
lesson: Pursuing formal clinical validation and health-system approvals let an AI chatbot startup survive where less rigorous competitors like Woebot's consumer app did not.

name: Youper
summary: Youper built an AI-powered emotional health assistant app that reached millions of users and maintained profitability through a lean, subscription-based consumer model.
lesson: Staying capital-efficient and reaching profitability early insulated Youper from the funding contraction that killed several better-funded rivals.

name: Shine
summary: Shine built a meditation and self-care app with a strong focus on inclusivity and BIPOC audiences, and was acquired by Peloton in 2021 to expand its wellness content library.
lesson: A differentiated brand voice and underserved audience focus can lead to a strategic acquisition even in a crowded meditation app market.

name: Amwell
summary: Amwell built a broad telehealth platform including significant behavioral health services and, despite a declining stock price post-IPO, remains an established public telehealth company still serving major health systems.
lesson: Deep, long-term health system partnerships provided revenue durability even as the company's public valuation fell sharply from IPO highs.

name: Tava Health
summary: Tava Health built an employer-focused mental health benefits platform connecting employees directly with licensed therapists, expanding steadily across regional employer accounts.
lesson: Focusing on mid-market employers, an underserved segment relative to giants chasing Fortune 500 logos, gave Tava a defensible niche.

name: Brave Health
summary: Brave Health provides virtual behavioral health care specifically to Medicaid populations, filling a chronically underserved segment ignored by most cash-pay-focused telehealth competitors.
lesson: Specializing in Medicaid, a payer segment most venture-backed competitors avoided, created a defensible and mission-aligned niche.

name: Iris Telehealth
summary: Iris Telehealth staffs psychiatric clinicians into hospitals, community mental health centers and health systems facing psychiatrist shortages, growing steadily through B2B contracts rather than consumer marketing.
lesson: A staffing-augmentation model addressing the psychiatrist shortage generated durable enterprise revenue without expensive consumer acquisition costs.

name: Sanvello Health
funding: Acquired by Pear Therapeutics (2020), later divested
summary: Sanvello (formerly Pacifica) built a clinically-backed self-care app for anxiety and depression with strong health-plan partnerships, surviving as an independent product after its parent Pear Therapeutics went bankrupt in 2023.
lesson: A strong independent clinical evidence base allowed Sanvello's product to survive and be divested even after its corporate parent collapsed.

name: Path Mental Health
summary: Path built an insurance-covered therapy and psychiatry marketplace with a strong focus on rural and underserved geographies, and merged with Rula Health in 2023 to form a larger combined national platform.
lesson: Prioritizing underserved rural geographies for expansion helped Path build a business attractive enough to merge from a position of strength rather than distress.
source: Company funding announcements, Behavioral Health Business

name: Frame (Frame Therapy)
summary: Frame built software infrastructure for therapists to run and bill for group therapy sessions, growing by making a historically under-monetized care format easier to deliver and reimburse.
lesson: Digitizing an underserved format of care (group therapy) that incumbents ignored created a defensible niche with less direct competition.

name: Ours (formerly Feels)
summary: Ours offered guided relationship and couples therapy sessions but shut down in 2023 as the niche couples-therapy telehealth category failed to reach sufficient scale to sustain the venture-backed business.
lesson: A narrow sub-vertical of an already crowded telehealth category can be too small to support independent venture-scale growth.

name: Iyaz Health
summary: A small mental health scheduling and access startup that failed to gain traction and quietly shut down in 2022 amid intensifying competition from well-funded marketplaces like Headway and Alma.
lesson: Entering the therapist-marketplace category without a differentiated wedge against well-capitalized incumbents is extremely difficult.

name: Total Brain
summary: Total Brain (formerly MyBrainSolutions) offered a brain-health assessment and mental fitness platform for employers, but wound down operations around 2023 as it failed to differentiate against larger EAP and benefits competitors.
lesson: Employer wellness platforms without a clear clinical or outcomes differentiator get squeezed out by better-funded, broader-benefit competitors.

name: Freespira
summary: Freespira developed an FDA-cleared home-based breathing biofeedback device for panic disorder and PTSD, but struggled with reimbursement and adoption before ceasing meaningful operations by 2023.
lesson: Device-based digital therapeutics face a doubly hard commercialization path, needing both device manufacturing scale and clinical reimbursement.

name: Empower Behavioral Health Systems
summary: A regional virtual behavioral health startup that scaled quickly during the pandemic telehealth boom but shut down in 2021 when demand normalized and it could not compete against nationally scaled platforms.
lesson: Pandemic-era demand spikes attracted regional operators that could not survive once national platforms with more capital moved into the same markets.

name: MoodGYM
summary: MoodGYM, an early Australian-university-developed online CBT program for depression and anxiety, remained in continuous use for over two decades as one of the first widely validated digital mental health interventions.
lesson: Academic-backed, publicly funded digital interventions can achieve remarkable longevity outside the venture-funding pressure cooker.

name: eMoods / Moodfit
summary: Moodfit, a self-guided mood-tracking and mental fitness app, failed to scale beyond a small user base and effectively ceased active development around 2022 amid competition from better-funded meditation and mood apps.
lesson: Bootstrapped consumer mental wellness apps struggle to compete on marketing spend against venture-backed category leaders.

name: Flow Neuroscience
summary: Flow Neuroscience developed a CE-marked at-home transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) headset for depression, gaining regulatory approval in Europe and building a growing direct-to-consumer device business.
lesson: Securing regulatory clearance in a friendlier market (Europe) before tackling the harder US FDA pathway let Flow build revenue and clinical evidence in parallel.

name: Koa Health
summary: Koa Health, spun out of Telefonica's research division, built enterprise mental health and wellbeing tools for employers and health systems across Europe and the US.
lesson: Spinning out of a large corporate R&D division gave Koa Health early clinical validation and enterprise relationships other startups had to build from scratch.

name: Innerwell
summary: Innerwell built a telehealth platform for ketamine-assisted psychotherapy and integrative mental health care, sustaining growth through a physician-led, insurance-adjacent model.
lesson: A lean, physician-led telehealth model for ketamine therapy avoided the heavy real-estate costs that sank clinic-based competitors.

name: Journey Clinical
summary: Journey Clinical built a B2B infrastructure platform enabling licensed therapists to offer ketamine-assisted psychotherapy under physician oversight, growing by empowering existing therapists rather than replacing them.
lesson: An infrastructure/enablement model for existing therapists avoided the capital intensity of directly operating psychedelic therapy clinics.

name: Compass Pathways
summary: Compass Pathways, a public company developing psilocybin therapy for treatment-resistant depression, has seen its stock price collapse over 90% from IPO highs as trials progressed slower than investors expected and commercialization remains years away.
lesson: Psychedelic drug development timelines routinely outlast public-market investor patience, punishing even scientifically credible programs.

name: MindLeaps / MyMD - Sanofi behavioral (placeholder removed)
summary: Placeholder entry removed from final dataset.
lesson: Placeholder removed.
